structured_choices.data_source: Retrospective cohort of 10,000 cancer patients from a major research hospital spanning 5 years (2019–2024). All patients have concurrent genomic profiling (gene expression RNA-seq or microarray, somatic mutation calls from exome/panel sequencing) and linked EHR records (demographics, diagnoses, treatment administrations, clinical assessments, labs). Data ingestion includes genomic data generated within 90 days of treatment initiation and EHR data from 180 days pre-treatment through 12 months post-treatment initiation.
structured_choices.label_definition: Treatment response is defined using RECIST 1.1 criteria (radiologically assessed at 8–12 weeks post-treatment initiation) and clinical assessor notes. Four mutually-exclusive primary labels: Complete Response (CR), Partial Response (PR), Stable Disease (SD), Progressive Disease (PD). Multi-label formulation: binary labels for each treatment type (e.g., 'responds_to_chemotherapy', 'responds_to_immunotherapy', 'responds_to_targeted_therapy') derived from patient treatment history and documented response outcomes. Patients are labeled only for treatments they actually received. Labels are adjudicated independently by two clinicians; disagreements are resolved by consensus review or excluded if unresolvable.
structured_choices.split_method: Stratified time-based split with patient-level grouping. Patients are chronologically ordered by treatment initiation date. The cohort is divided into three non-overlapping temporal blocks: (1) Training: patients from 2019–2021 (n≈6,000), (2) Validation: patients from 2021–2022 (n≈2,000), (3) Test: patients from 2022–2024 (n≈2,000). Within each block, stratification is performed on: (a) cancer type (stratify to maintain ~3–5 major cancer types in each fold), (b) treatment category (ensure each treatment type appears in train/val/test in similar proportions), and (c) treatment response distribution (balance CR/PR/SD/PD across splits). This temporal split prevents information leakage (future treatment outcomes cannot inform the model), while stratification ensures consistent label balance and clinical diversity across splits. Patient-level grouping ensures no single patient appears across splits.
structured_choices.preprocessing_fit_point: All preprocessing transformers (scaling, imputation, encoding, dimensionality reduction) are fit exclusively on the training set and applied identically to validation and test sets. Genomic feature engineering (e.g., log2 transformation, combat batch correction, gene set aggregation) is fit on training genomic data only. Missing EHR values are imputed using training-set-derived means/medians or k-NN (k=5, fit on training data). One-hot encoding of categorical EHR features is fit on training cardinalities. Genomic variance filtering (retain genes with variance > 25th percentile in training set) is determined from training data. No information from validation or test sets informs any transformation.
structured_choices.features: Genomic features: (1) Log2-transformed gene expression (top 500 genes by variance in training set, selected to reduce noise and computational cost while retaining signal), (2) Binary somatic mutation indicators (one-hot encoded for recurrent mutations appearing in ≥2% of training cohort), (3) Mutation burden (total non-synonymous mutations per patient). EHR features: (1) Demographics (age, sex, race/ethnicity, BMI), (2) Tumor characteristics (cancer type, stage at diagnosis, histology), (3) Treatment history (prior lines of therapy, prior treatment responses, time since last treatment), (4) Comorbidities (CHARLSON index components), (5) Laboratory values (most recent hemoglobin, albumin, creatinine, LDH within 30 days pre-treatment). Derived features: (1) Gene expression–mutation interaction terms (product of top 10 variance genes × binary mutation status for high-impact mutations), (2) EHR–genomic composite: genomic risk score (logistic regression on genomic features alone, fit on training data) crossed with treatment type. Total feature dimensionality: ~550 features (500 genomic + 30 EHR + 20 interaction/derived).
structured_choices.model: Multi-task deep learning model with shared lower layers and task-specific output heads. Architecture: (1) Input layer (550 features), (2) Batch normalization, (3) Three shared dense layers (256→128→64 units, ReLU activation, 20% dropout after each), (4) Three task-specific output heads (one per treatment type: chemotherapy, immunotherapy, targeted therapy), each with one dense layer (32 units, ReLU) and sigmoid output (binary classification per treatment). Task-specific weights in loss function are inversely proportional to label prevalence (down-weight common labels, up-weight rare ones). Model is trained end-to-end with multi-task binary cross-entropy loss (unweighted sum of per-task losses). Optimizer: Adam (learning rate 1e-3). Early stopping on validation metric (see below) with patience=15 epochs.
structured_choices.baseline: Gradient Boosting baseline (XGBoost) trained on the same feature set with identical preprocessing and train/val/test splits. XGBoost is a strong, interpretable baseline appropriate for structured heterogeneous data. Baseline receives equivalent hyperparameter tuning effort: random search over 50 configurations on the validation set, tuning max_depth, learning_rate, subsample, colsample_bytree, and regularization (L1/L2). Baseline is evaluated under the same multi-task formulation: three independent binary classifiers (one per treatment type), predictions aggregated identically to the deep learning model. This ensures fair comparison on the hypothesis of whether the multi-task deep learning model exploits shared genomic-EHR structure more effectively than independent gradient boosting.
structured_choices.primary_metric: Macro-averaged F1 score across the three treatment types (chemotherapy, immunotherapy, targeted therapy), computed on the test set. Justification: (1) Treatment response prediction is operationally meaningful when the model correctly identifies both responders and non-responders; F1 balances precision and recall, avoiding false positives (costly mis-allocations of treatment) and false negatives (missed treatment opportunities). (2) Macro-averaging weights each treatment type equally, reflecting the clinical goal of generalizable performance across treatment modalities, not performance on the most common treatment alone. (3) This metric aligns with stakeholder intent: identify patients likely to benefit from *specific* treatments, requiring per-treatment prediction accuracy. (4) F1 is threshold-agnostic, appropriate for a research setting where the deployment threshold may be tuned by clinicians post-hoc.
secondary_metrics: Per-treatment-type F1 scores (F1 for chemotherapy, immunotherapy, targeted therapy separately) to detect heterogeneous performance.
Macro-averaged AUROC to assess rank-order discrimination independent of threshold.
Per-label precision and recall (macro-averaged) to identify systematic bias (e.g., over-predicting responders).
Calibration: Brier score and Expected Calibration Error (ECE) binned by deciles, important for clinician trust in predicted probabilities.
Subgroup performance: F1, AUROC stratified by cancer type, age group, and prior treatment lines to detect fairness issues or confounding.
structured_choices.model_selection_data: Hyperparameter tuning for both the multi-task deep learning model and XGBoost baseline uses the validation set (n≈2,000). For the deep learning model: random search over 40 configurations, tuning shared layer widths (128–256), dropout rates (10–30%), learning rates (1e-4 to 1e-2), task-specific layer widths (16–64), and loss function weighting schemes. Validation metric is macro-averaged F1 on the validation set. Best model is selected as the configuration with highest validation F1. Early stopping uses validation F1 with patience=15 epochs to prevent overfitting. The test set is held out and never used for tuning.
structured_choices.test_set_policy: The test set (patients from 2022–2024, n≈2,000) is evaluated exactly once, after model selection is complete. All reported metrics (primary and secondary) are computed on the test set. No hyperparameters or feature selections are modified based on test performance. Confidence intervals (95%, via bootstrap resampling with 1,000 iterations stratified by treatment type) are reported around all primary and secondary metrics on the test set.
structured_choices.confound_controls: Treatment protocol variability and patient compliance are potential confounds. Mitigation: (1) Include treatment protocol version/hospital location as a stratification variable in train/val/test splits to ensure consistent protocol distribution across sets. (2) Include prior treatment lines and treatment sequencing in EHR features—the model can learn to adjust for protocol switching. (3) Proxy for compliance: include time-to-imaging follow-up (patients with delayed imaging may have worse compliance); include in EHR features. (4) Subgroup analysis: compute F1 separately for patients stratified by treatment protocol version and institution to verify performance is robust, not driven by unmeasured protocol confounds. (5) Sensitivity analysis: retrain the model excluding patients with missing imaging follow-up or >180-day delays to quantify impact of compliance-related missing data. The experiment establishes that the multi-task deep learning model outperforms the baseline under the data conditions tested; it does not establish causality or rule out all unmeasured confounds (e.g., tumor heterogeneity not captured by genomic profiling). Scope of claim: 'Within this retrospective cohort, incorporating both genomic and EHR features via multi-task learning improves treatment response prediction compared to gradient boosting on EHR features alone.'

design_narrative:
## Experimental Design: Multi-Task Deep Learning for Cancer Treatment Response Prediction

### 1. Data Source and Label Definition

The experiment uses a retrospective cohort of 10,000 cancer patients from a major research hospital spanning 2019–2024. Each patient has concurrent genomic profiling (gene expression and somatic mutation calls generated within 90 days of treatment initiation) and linked EHR records covering 180 days pre-treatment through 12 months post-treatment. 

Treatment response labels are defined using RECIST 1.1 criteria at 8–12 weeks post-treatment initiation. To support the multi-label prediction task, we derive three binary labels per patient (one for each treatment modality): responds_to_chemotherapy, responds_to_immunotherapy, responds_to_targeted_therapy. Patients are labeled only for treatments they received. All labels are adjudicated independently by two clinicians; disagreements are resolved by consensus or excluded if unresolvable.

### 2. Data Split Strategy

We employ a stratified temporal split to prevent information leakage while maintaining clinical diversity. The cohort is divided chronologically by treatment initiation date into three non-overlapping blocks: training (2019–2021, n≈6,000), validation (2021–2022, n≈2,000), and test (2022–2024, n≈2,000). Within each block, we stratify on three dimensions: (a) cancer type (to ensure major cancer types appear proportionally in all splits), (b) treatment category (to balance chemotherapy, immunotherapy, targeted therapy across splits), and (c) treatment response distribution (to maintain label balance). This temporal split prevents the model from learning future information, while stratification ensures consistent demographic and clinical distributions across folds. Patient-level grouping ensures no single patient appears in multiple splits.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

All preprocessing transformers are fit exclusively on the training set and applied identically to validation and test data. Genomic features include log2-transformed gene expression (top 500 genes by variance in training data, selected to reduce noise), binary somatic mutation indicators (one-hot encoded for mutations appearing in ≥2% of training samples), and mutation burden. EHR features include demographics, tumor stage, treatment history, comorbidity indices, and recent laboratory values. We construct 20 derived interaction features (e.g., gene expression × mutation status for high-impact mutations) and a genomic risk score (logistic regression on genomic features alone, fit on training data) crossed with treatment type. Total dimensionality: ~550 features. Missing EHR values are imputed using training-set-derived means/medians or k-NN (k=5); no validation or test information informs imputation.

### 4. Model and Baseline

The proposed model is a multi-task deep learning architecture with shared lower layers and three task-specific output heads (one per treatment type). The model has three shared dense layers (256→128→64 units, ReLU activation, 20% dropout), followed by task-specific layers (32-unit dense, sigmoid output). Loss is multi-task binary cross-entropy with task-specific weighting inversely proportional to label prevalence. The baseline is XGBoost trained on identical features, preprocessing, and train/val/test splits. Both models receive equivalent hyperparameter tuning effort via random search on the validation set (50 configurations for XGBoost, 40 for deep learning). This ensures fair comparison of the hypothesis: does multi-task deep learning better exploit shared genomic-EHR structure than standard gradient boosting?

### 5. Evaluation and Model Selection

The primary metric is macro-averaged F1 score across the three treatment types, computed on the held-out test set. F1 balances precision and recall—critical for avoiding both false-positive mis-allocations of treatment and false-negative missed opportunities. Macro-averaging reflects the clinical goal of generalizable performance across all treatment modalities. Secondary metrics include per-treatment F1, macro-averaged AUROC, calibration metrics (Brier score, ECE), and subgroup performance stratified by cancer type, age, and prior treatment lines. 

Model selection uses the validation set. Hyperparameters are tuned to maximize validation F1. Early stopping is applied during training with patience=15 epochs. The test set is held out and evaluated exactly once after model selection completes.

### 6. Controls and Confound Mitigation

Treatment protocol variability and patient compliance are potential confounds. We mitigate these by: (1) stratifying train/val/test by treatment protocol version to ensure consistent protocol distribution, (2) including protocol and compliance proxies in EHR features, (3) performing subgroup analyses by protocol version to verify robustness, and (4) conducting sensitivity analyses excluding patients with delayed imaging follow-up. The experiment establishes whether multi-task deep learning outperforms the baseline within this cohort under the tested conditions; it does not establish causality or eliminate all unmeasured confounds.
